Topic 11: Why this is justified

[268:57]And I hate to say it, but I predicted this. So as you know, the United States went into

[269:07]in an operation effectively to extradite

[269:12]the president of Venezuela, Nicholas Maduro.

[269:15]Now, at that time, within this space,

[269:18]this was very controversial.

[269:21]And there were some language problems.

[269:23]There were some semantic confusion.

[269:26]People characterized that operation as a regime change,

[269:30]as a war.

[269:32]But when you look at what happened in Venezuela,

[269:35]In retrospect, now five months have gone by. We can look at it with clear eyes here. What

[269:42]precisely happened in January when the United States special forces moved in and kidnapped

[269:49]the president of Venezuela? Well, it was not a war.

[269:53]It was not.

[269:55]There were no major kinetic operations,

[269:58]no ongoing hostilities.

[270:00]We didn't lose any equipment.

[270:02]We didn't lose any men.

[270:03]We barely suffered any injuries.

[270:06]On their side, there were fewer than a hundred casualties.

[270:11]We kidnapped their leader

[270:12]and that effectively ended the hostilities.

[270:15]It was not long and drawn out. It was not protracted. There were not any

[270:21]escalating actions, no other operations. It cost us virtually nothing.

[270:25]It wasn't a war.

[270:28]By the way, it wasn't even a regime change because to this day,

[270:32]Nicholas Maduro's vice president, his security minister, his interior minister, they all remain in their positions.

[270:41]What does that mean? It means that the political and security apparatus of the Maduro Chavista regime

[270:47]remain fully intact.

[270:49]So it's the same military, the same cops, same personnel, same civilian government. It's the same operation.

[270:57]They are just being treated like a colony, effectively,

[271:02]governed indirectly by an American viceroy, which looks to be

[271:07]energy secretary Chris Wright.

[271:10]Or something like this, maybe Marco Rubio to some extent?

[271:14]And I will say that in retrospect,

[271:17]I think that is actually a model of what soft

[271:21]or arguably smart American power projection

[271:25]can look like in the world.

[271:27]Let's be honest.

[271:28]We have the biggest military.

[271:30]We have a considerable military advantage

[271:33]over every other country.

[271:34]And on occasion, it is beneficial to us

[271:37]and our interest to use it.

[271:41]And that is just politics.

[271:44]Now, a lot of people are very apprehensive about this idea.

[271:48]People describe themselves as ideological non-interventionists

[271:52]or isolationists.

[271:54]Or they think that America first means something like that.

[271:57]But typically when they say they don't like intervention

[272:01]or they think intervention is almost never justified,

[272:04]they'll bring up the legacy of the war in Iraq.

[272:06]And what are the problems with that?

[272:09]Well, the problems of the war in Iraq are that it was expensive.

[272:14]Too many people died.

[272:15]It went on for too long.

[272:16]There wasn't a clear objective.

[272:18]It's debatable whether it actually benefited us and in what ways it was on the other side

[272:24]of the globe.

[272:25]So because of proximity, how could it possibly affect US national security or the security

[272:31]of the homeland directly?

[272:33]But that does not apply.

[272:34]Those questions and concerns and criticisms don't apply to every intervention, this one

[272:40]in particular.

[272:42]In Venezuela, it was not expensive.

[272:45]Nobody was hurt or died.

[272:47]It was not long and drawn out.

[272:50]There were clear objectives and they were met.

[272:52]There was no mission creep.

[272:53]And there's a very articulable and clear and objective benefit for the United States.

[273:03]So let's just clear the air.

[273:05]In retrospect, I was right about Venezuela.

[273:09]There were all these people saying, oh, this has something to do with Israel.

[273:13]And people were saying, oh, this is another forever war.

[273:16]Oh, this is a betrayal of Trump's promises.

[273:19]I don't think so.

[273:20]I think that fits neatly within the Trump doctrine, a Jacksonian foreign policy enforcing

[273:33]and pursuing legitimate American interests where it is possible.

[273:38]Look, Venezuela's in our neighborhood.

[273:40]It's not like Iran, which is on the other side of the planet.

[273:44]What's more, Venezuela is not a middle power like Iran is.

[273:50]Venezuela is not rich, does not control a vital choke point, does not have any strategic

[273:55]arsenal of conventional or non-conventional weapons, doesn't have a substantial military.

[274:01]And by the way, they're hosting rival nations militaries in our sphere of influence, which

[274:10]is the entire Western hemisphere.

[274:14]Totally the right thing to do, cost us nothing, gained us something.

[274:19]And one of the big legacies of the intervention in Venezuela is that of course it was never

[274:23]just about Venezuela, this was a part of our new National Defense Strategy, National Security

[274:31]Strategy promulgated by the Pentagon earlier in the year.

[274:35]And what was our new strategic outlook under the second Trump administration?

[274:41]Well, the rewritten playbook said that our new priority will not be Europe.

[274:47]countering and balancing against Russia.

[274:50]It will not be the Middle East guaranteeing the security of Israel and balancing against

[274:56]Iran.

[275:01]invasion of Taiwan and building up our alliances with the Quad and other Pacific allies, rather

[275:13]They said, actually, we're going to prioritize the neglected region of the Caribbean and

[275:19]Latin America.

[275:22]They say that what has been happening over the past 15 years is that China has supplanted

[275:27]the United States as the number one trading partner of all the Latin American countries.

[275:40]specifically dual use infrastructure like deep water ports, radar dishes, in some cases

[275:47]space stations, military installations that could in the future be converted to a strategic

[276:02]and the biggest cargo ships. It could also one day in the future host an aircraft carrier

[276:37]Naval and seaborne activity, whether commercial or military,

[276:41]in what should be American waters, what's more.

[276:45]In the era of missiles and missile defense,

[276:49]you can actually conceal a missile launch system

[276:53]inside of a container.

[276:55]inside a shipping container that you might see

[277:12]on a cargo ship docked in Haiti,

[277:17]where our vital shipping lanes are.

Topic 12: Expanding the empire & making a deal

[277:30]We went overseas to fight Russia, to fight Iran, to fight Iraq, in some cases even to

[277:35]fight China in their home turf.

[277:38]And what has happened in the meantime is we've lost our home.

[277:42]China has made this economic incursion, which is rapidly becoming something like a military

[277:48]into our hemisphere. And the whole basis of American foreign policy is that we are always

[277:54]safe because we don't have to worry about our hemisphere. There are no threats here.

[277:59]Well, that doesn't work if China and Russia have a foothold in this area. So in any case,

[278:06]we invaded a rather extradited Maduro in Venezuela as part of this new grand strategy.

[278:12]But it wasn't just about Venezuela. It was about changing the whole dynamic here.

[278:18]And the next step in this domino theory is that if we took Venezuela out of the picture,

[278:24]if we took them offline, then the next to fall would be Cuba.

[278:30]There is this axis in Central America, the Caribbean and Latin America, which consists

[278:37]in Venezuela, Cuba, Nicaragua, previously Bolivia.

[278:41]These were all far left governments, socialist governments in league with Russia and China,

[278:47]formerly the Soviet Union that had military partnerships with Russia, economic partnerships

[278:52]with China, which were opposed to U.S. hegemony.

[278:59]And so the idea is we take down Venezuela, Cuba falls next, and then Nicaragua.

[279:05]And once this happens, then the Western Hemisphere becomes the uncontested domain of the American

[279:12]Empire.

[279:13]That's the idea.

[279:15]And the way that this would be achieved is that Cuba is totally dependent on Venezuela

[279:19]and Mexico for its oil.

[279:21]Cuba is an island.

[279:23]They don't have any native energy production.

[279:25]They have to import all their energy, literally all of it.

[279:30]And it's all coming from Mexico, our largest trading partner, and Venezuela.

[279:35]So the idea is that if we can control Venezuela's oil exports, if we can lean on Mexico, then

[279:42]we can effectively shut down all of Cuba's energy. And if Cuba doesn't have energy,

[279:48]then they don't have a country. You don't have oil and diesel. Well, you don't have

[279:53]transportation and you don't have electricity. You don't have air conditioning. You don't

[280:09]be strangled and they will have to beg to negotiate and they will have to work out some kind of

[280:14]a deal with the United States.

[280:16]Well, it looks like that is now working.

[280:20]And I don't want to say that it's a done deal, but it looks like that strategy is now bearing

[280:25]fruit.

[280:30]Their president came out and said literally we got nothing. We're out. We have no diesel. We have no nothing

[280:38]He said and now the blackouts can be expected to last 20 to 22 hours per day

[280:44]You're not gonna have electricity outside of two maybe four hours every day. That's it. We're running on fumes here

Topic 14: A new American empire & clip-farming
